other_side: Where NLW frames Anthropic as a firm with unprecedented, almost sovereign power over the future, Zitron paints a company propping up its numbers. He argues its lone 'profitable' quarter is an accounting artefact, timed to a heavily discounted compute deal with SpaceX over Colossus and released to coincide with NVIDIA's earnings day to 'keep the AI bubble inflated.' On his reading costs still scale linearly with revenue, so the supposed gatekeeper is less an emperor than a heavily subsidised tenant that cannot yet stand on its own books.
truth_beneath: The single variable both men orbit is whether Anthropic's power is durable or borrowed. Both accept that Anthropic is, for now, setting terms others must accept, from Fable's guardrails to its retention policy. The empirical question is whether that leverage is backed by self-sustaining economics or by subsidised compute and bubble capital, and the observable that settles it is audited GAAP financials plus whether enterprise customers actually stay after the data-retention furore. The residue is worldview: NLW sees a lab with structural power over the economy; Zitron sees a cash-burning firm one funding round from discipline. NLW is vindicated if Anthropic keeps dictating terms and customers swallow them; Zitron is vindicated if the walk-backs, Microsoft's restrictions and the economics force Anthropic to compete on price and openness. Both are polemicists with incentives, NLW bullish and anti-doom while selling adoption, Zitron bubble-certain and anti-hype while selling the autopsy, so the signal, despite opposite priors, is that this week's behaviour read as insecurity, not confidence. Watch enterprise retention and Anthropic's first audited numbers post-IPO.
